lesson_title: Finding issues before they become problems

cards.front: Why should you always compile with warnings enabled?
cards.back: Compiler warnings catch many potential bugs before they become runtime problems. They identify suspicious code patterns that are legal but likely incorrect.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.text: Turn on compiler warnings with {{c1::-Wall -Weffc++ -Wextra}} (GCC/Clang) or {{c2::/W4}} (Visual Studio).
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.front: What is the 'treat warnings as errors' compiler setting?
cards.back: A compiler option that makes the build fail if there are any warnings, forcing you to fix them before the program can be built.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.text: Enable 'treat warnings as errors' with {{c1::-Werror}} (GCC/Clang) or {{c2::/WX}} (Visual Studio).
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.concept: Benefits of treating warnings as errors
cards.explanation:
Treating warnings as errors:
  • Prevents ignoring warnings
  • Forces immediate fixes
  • Catches bugs early in development
  • Maintains clean codebase
  • Makes warnings visible to whole team
examples: // With -Werror: build fails, must fix now
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.front: What is a linter or static analyzer?
cards.back: A tool that analyzes source code without running it, looking for bugs, style issues, and suspicious patterns beyond what the compiler checks.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.text: Popular C++ static analyzers include {{c1::cppcheck}}, {{c2::clang-tidy}}, and {{c3::PVS-Studio}}.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.front: What is the purpose of unit testing?
cards.back: Testing individual functions or components in isolation to verify they work correctly before integrating them into the larger program.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.explanation:
Prevent bugs before they occur:
  • Enable and fix all compiler warnings
  • Use static analyzers/linters
  • Write unit tests
  • Use assertions to validate assumptions
  • Initialize all variables
  • Use const correctness
  • Code reviews
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.question: What warning would a good compiler give for this code?
cards.answer: Warning: 'x' is used uninitialized. The variable has no value but is being returned.
cards.explanation: This is a common bug that compiler warnings catch - using a variable before giving it a value.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.front: What's the advantage of catching bugs with static analysis vs. debugging at runtime?
cards.back: Static analysis finds bugs before you even run the code, saving time. Runtime debugging requires reproducing the bug, which may be difficult or time-consuming.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.text: The best way to fix bugs is to {{c1::prevent}} them from happening in the first place through good coding practices and {{c2::tools}}.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.text: {{c1::Static analyzers}} examine code without {{c2::running}} it, finding bugs and suspicious patterns.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems

cards.question: What warning would this trigger with -Wall?
cards.answer: Warning: unused variable 'result' (or 'parameter y unused' depending on the compiler).
cards.explanation: The compiler notices result is assigned but never used, suggesting a logic error.
cards.source: 3.10 - Finding issues before they become problems
